prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We need to get to the bottom of this issue.
correct: We need to investigate this issue thoroughly.
We need to determine the root cause of this issue.
explanation: <Get to the bottom of> → <investigate thoroughly> or <determine the root cause> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The new policy is a step in the right direction.
correct: The new policy represents a positive development.
The new policy constitutes a positive advancement.
explanation: <A step in the right direction> → <a positive development> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We shouldn't put all our eggs in one basket.
correct: We should diversify our approach.
We should not rely on a single strategy.
We shouldn't rely on a single strategy.
explanation: <Put all eggs in one basket> → <rely on a single strategy> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The project is back to square one.
correct: The project must be restarted from the beginning.
The project has returned to its initial stage.
explanation: <Back to square one> → <restarted from the beginning> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: She hit the nail on the head with her analysis.
correct: Her analysis was precisely accurate.
She identified the issue exactly.
explanation: <Hit the nail on the head> → <precisely accurate> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We need to think outside the box to solve this.
correct: We need to adopt an innovative approach to solve this.
We need to consider unconventional solutions.
explanation: <Think outside the box> → <adopt an innovative approach> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The company is in hot water over the scandal.
correct: The company is facing serious difficulties over the scandal.
The company is in a precarious situation due to the scandal.
explanation: <In hot water> → <facing serious difficulties> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: Let's not beat around the bush — the results are disappointing.
correct: To be direct, the results are disappointing.
Speaking frankly, the results are disappointing.
explanation: <Beat around the bush> → <to be direct/speaking frankly> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The new regulations have opened a can of worms.
correct: The new regulations have created unforeseen complications.
The new regulations have raised numerous complex issues.
explanation: <Opened a can of worms> → <created unforeseen complications> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We need to keep our eye on the ball during negotiations.
correct: We need to maintain focus during negotiations.
We need to remain attentive during negotiations.
explanation: <Keep our eye on the ball> → <maintain focus> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The CEO pulled strings to get the deal approved.
correct: The CEO used his influence to get the deal approved.
The CEO leveraged his connections to get the deal approved.
explanation: <Pull strings> → <use influence/leverage connections> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We're barking up the wrong tree with this approach.
correct: We are pursuing an incorrect line of inquiry.
Our current approach is misguided.
We're pursuing an incorrect line of inquiry.
explanation: <Barking up the wrong tree> → <pursuing an incorrect line of inquiry> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The merger is still up in the air.
correct: The merger remains undecided.
The merger has not yet been finalised.
The merger hasn't yet been finalised.
explanation: <Up in the air> → <remains undecided> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: She went above and beyond to complete the project.
correct: She exceeded expectations to complete the project.
She demonstrated exceptional dedication to complete the project.
explanation: <Go above and beyond> → <exceed expectations> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The team needs to get their act together before the deadline.
correct: The team needs to improve their organisation before the deadline.
The team must become more coordinated before the deadline.
explanation: <Get their act together> → <improve organisation> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The budget cuts are just the tip of the iceberg.
correct: The budget cuts represent only a fraction of the larger problem.
The budget cuts are merely indicative of more extensive issues.
explanation: <Tip of the iceberg> → <a fraction of the larger problem> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We need to nip this problem in the bud.
correct: We need to address this problem at its earliest stage.
We need to resolve this issue before it escalates.
explanation: <Nip in the bud> → <address at its earliest stage> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The new manager has turned the company around.
correct: The new manager has revitalised the company.
The new manager has transformed the company's fortunes.
explanation: <Turn around> → <revitalise/transform> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We're all in the same boat regarding the pay freeze.
correct: We are all equally affected by the pay freeze.
We all face the same circumstances regarding the pay freeze.
We're all equally affected by the pay freeze.
explanation: <In the same boat> → <equally affected> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The proposal was shot down by the board.
correct: The proposal was rejected by the board.
The board dismissed the proposal.
explanation: <Shot down> → <rejected/dismissed> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: She has a lot on her plate at the moment.
correct: She has numerous responsibilities at the moment.
She is currently managing a heavy workload.
She's currently managing a heavy workload.
explanation: <A lot on her plate> → <numerous responsibilities> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The negotiations have reached a dead end.
correct: The negotiations have reached an impasse.

explanation: <Dead end> → <impasse> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: We need to read between the lines of this report.
correct: We need to interpret the implicit meaning of this report.

explanation: <Read between the lines> → <interpret the implicit meaning> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: The company is cutting corners to save money.
correct: The company is reducing quality standards to save money.
The company is compromising on standards to reduce costs.
explanation: <Cutting corners> → <reducing quality standards> in formal register.

prompt: Replace the idiom with a formal equivalent
source: He always passes the buck when things go wrong.
correct: He always deflects responsibility when things go wrong.

explanation: <Pass the buck> → <deflect responsibility> in formal register.
